questions.stem: A patient has two complaints: a sharply bordered red plaque with thick silvery scale on the elbow, and separate patches of red, ill-defined, oozy, intensely itchy skin in the elbow creases. How are these two patterns best categorized?
options: The elbow plaque is papulosquamous; the itchy oozy flexural rash is dermatitic
The flexural rash is papulosquamous; the plaque is dermatitic
questions.explain: Well-demarcated + scale = papulosquamous (the silvery plaque, psoriasis). Ill-defined + itchy/oozy = dermatitic (the flexural eczema). This split organizes the red-and-scaly differential.

guide.s: The first split: papulosquamous vs dermatitic

questions.stem: A man with stable plaque psoriasis develops a marked flare a few weeks after starting a new medication for his bipolar disorder. Which drug most likely triggered the flare?
questions.explain: Classic psoriasis drug triggers are beta-blockers, lithium, NSAIDs, antimalarials, and systemic steroid withdrawal. Other triggers: streptococcal infection, stress, trauma (Koebner), cold, smoking/alcohol.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old with atopic dermatitis has recurrent flares on the eyelids and around the mouth. The parents are anxious about repeatedly using topical steroids on the face. What is the most appropriate steroid-sparing option for these sensitive areas?
questions.explain: Topical calcineurin inhibitors are well suited to the face/eyelids and flexures and for maintenance, avoiding steroid atrophy. Daily emollients remain the backbone; low-potency steroids are used for short flares.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old with known eczema develops, over 24 hours, clusters of painful, uniform-looking small vesicles and punched-out erosions over the face and neck, with fever and malaise. What is the diagnosis and the immediate priority?
options: Impetiginized eczema; start topical mupirocin and continue emollients
Eczema herpeticum; start urgent systemic antiviral therapy and assess for eye involvement
An eczema flare; increase the topical steroid potency
Allergic contact dermatitis; begin patch testing
questions.explain: Painful, monomorphic vesicles / punched-out erosions, rapid spread, and fever in an atopic patient = eczema herpeticum (disseminated HSV). It needs urgent systemic antivirals and ophthalmology review if peri-ocular — not just topical care. Honey crusting would instead suggest bacterial impetiginization.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old develops itchy, linear streaks of vesicles and bullae on the forearms two days after clearing brush in her garden. What mechanism and confirmatory test fit best?
options: Immediate IgE-mediated (type I) reaction; serum tryptase
Delayed type IV hypersensitivity; patch testing
questions.explain: Linear vesicles in an exposure pattern after plant contact (e.g. poison ivy) is allergic contact dermatitis, a delayed type IV hypersensitivity. The allergen is identified by patch testing; treat with avoidance + topical steroids (systemic if widespread).

questions.stem: A healthy 22-year-old noticed a single 3 cm scaly oval patch on the trunk, then a week later developed many smaller salmon-coloured oval plaques along skin-cleavage lines in a 'Christmas-tree' pattern, sparing the face. What is the best management?
options: Reassurance — it is self-limited over 6–8 weeks
questions.explain: A herald patch followed by a Christmas-tree distribution with collarette scale is pityriasis rosea (linked to HHV-6/7), self-limited over 6–8 weeks. Management is reassurance; symptomatic care doesn't change the course.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old has intensely itchy, violaceous, flat-topped polygonal papules on the wrists and shins, with fine white lacy lines on the surface and similar lacy change on the buccal mucosa. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: The 6 Ps — pruritic, purple, polygonal, peripheral, papules (± penile) — with Wickham striae and oral mucosal involvement define lichen planus. Manage with topical steroids and evaluate mucosa/nails/scalp.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old has several erosions with golden honey-coloured crusts around the nose and mouth. He is otherwise well, and the lesions are limited. What is the best first-line treatment?
options: Topical mupirocin (or fusidic acid) after gently removing crusts
questions.explain: Honey-coloured crusts = impetigo (usually S. aureus). For limited disease, remove crusts and apply topical mupirocin or fusidic acid; reserve oral antibiotics (cloxacillin/cephalosporin) for extensive disease.

questions.stem: A diabetic man has a rapidly spreading red, swollen leg over 12 hours. He reports pain far out of proportion to the modest skin findings, is febrile and tachycardic, and now has a dusky patch with a bulla. What is the priority?
options: Urgent surgical evaluation for debridement plus broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
questions.explain: Pain out of proportion, rapid progression, systemic toxicity, and dusky skin/bullae signal necrotizing fasciitis — a surgical emergency. Give broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and obtain immediate surgical debridement; imaging/labs must not delay surgery.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old has a patch of hair loss with scaling, broken-off hairs, and tender posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. KOH is positive. What is the appropriate treatment principle?
questions.explain: Tinea capitis involves the hair follicle/shaft, so topical therapy is inadequate — it requires oral antifungal. Lymphadenopathy and broken hairs are classic clues.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old notices several oval, faintly scaly patches on the upper back and shoulders that fail to tan with the rest of his skin in summer. KOH shows short hyphae and spores. What is the diagnosis?
questions.explain: Hypopigmented, finely scaly patches in a seborrheic distribution from Malassezia overgrowth = pityriasis versicolor; treat with topical antifungals (oral azole if extensive). Contrast vitiligo, which is fully depigmented, not hypopigmented, and non-scaly.

questions.stem: A university student has severe itch that is worst at night; two roommates are also itchy. Exam shows excoriated papules with subtle linear tracks in the finger webs and on the wrists. What is the best management principle?
options: Topical steroid alone for presumed eczema
Permethrin cream (or oral ivermectin) plus treatment of close contacts and laundering of clothing/linens
questions.explain: Nocturnal itch, burrows in finger webs/wrists, and affected contacts = scabies. Treat the patient with permethrin or ivermectin, treat close contacts, and decontaminate clothing/linens to prevent reinfestation.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman has central-face redness, flushing triggered by heat and wine, and scattered inflammatory papules and pustules — but no comedones. What single feature best separates her condition from acne?
questions.explain: Acne and rosacea both have papules/pustules, but acne has comedones and rosacea does not — the high-yield discriminator. Flushing and central-face involvement further favour rosacea.

questions.stem: A patient is about to start a systemic acne medication for severe scarring cystic acne. Which agent mandates a pregnancy-prevention program and baseline/monitoring β-hCG because it is strongly teratogenic?
questions.explain: Isotretinoin is a potent teratogen — it requires pregnancy prevention and β-hCG monitoring (plus lipids/LFTs) in patients who can become pregnant. It's reserved for severe cystic/scarring or refractory acne.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman who is 10 weeks pregnant asks about treating her moderate inflammatory acne. Which commonly used acne therapy is contraindicated in pregnancy?
questions.explain: Oral tetracyclines (doxycycline, minocycline) are contraindicated in pregnancy and in children <8 (dental staining, skeletal effects); oral retinoids/isotretinoin and topical retinoids are also avoided. Benzoyl peroxide and gentle skin care are acceptable.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old fair-skinned man has a slowly enlarging pearly papule on the upper lip with surface telangiectasias and intermittent bleeding. What is the most likely diagnosis and its usual behaviour?
options: Basal cell carcinoma; locally destructive with rare metastasis
questions.explain: A pearly, telangiectatic, bleeding papule on sun-exposed skin is classic basal cell carcinoma — the most common cancer, locally destructive but rarely metastatic.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old has an asymmetric, irregularly bordered pigmented lesion on the calf that has enlarged and changed colour over 8 months. Which approach is correct, and what most determines prognosis?
options: Shave biopsy; lesion diameter determines prognosis
Excisional biopsy to obtain Breslow depth; depth and ulceration most determine prognosis
Topical steroid trial; symmetry determines prognosis
questions.explain: A changing, asymmetric pigmented lesion needs excisional biopsy (not shave/cryo) to measure Breslow depth. Breslow depth and ulceration are the dominant prognostic factors and drive wide local excision margins and staging.

questions.stem: Five weeks after starting an anticonvulsant, a patient develops fever, facial swelling, a widespread morbilliform rash, lymphadenopathy, and bloodwork showing eosinophilia and elevated transaminases. What is the most likely reaction?
options: DRESS (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms)
questions.explain: Onset 2–8 weeks after a drug, with fever, facial edema, eosinophilia, and organ involvement (hepatitis), is DRESS/DHS. Stop the culprit, check CBC/diff, LFTs, Cr, UA; systemic steroids for significant organ involvement with prolonged taper.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old has weeks of intense itch followed by tense, fluid-filled blisters on erythematous and urticarial plaques on the trunk and legs. The oral mucosa is spared. Which pathogenesis fits best?
options: Antibodies to the basement membrane causing a subepidermal blister
Antibodies to desmogleins causing intraepidermal acantholysis
Dermatophyte invasion of the stratum corneum
IgA deposition after a streptococcal infection
questions.explain: Tense bullae in an elderly, intensely pruritic patient with mucosal sparing = bullous pemphigoid — autoantibodies against the basement membrane → subepidermal blister, Nikolsky negative. Pemphigus vulgaris instead targets desmogleins (intraepidermal, flaccid bullae, oral erosions, Nikolsky positive).

questions.stem: A 3-month-old has a rapidly enlarging bright-red vascular plaque adjacent to the right lower eyelid that is beginning to obstruct the visual axis. What is the appropriate systemic therapy?
questions.explain: An infantile hemangioma threatening vision (a high-risk peri-ocular location) warrants treatment: oral propranolol is the typical systemic therapy. Most uncomplicated hemangiomas proliferate ~6 months then involute and need only observation.

cards.prompt: Match each primary lesion to its definition:
cards.explain: Size (1 cm) and whether raised/fluid/pus distinguish the primary lesions.

cards.text: An {{erosion}} is loss of epidermis and heals without a scar; an {{ulcer}} extends into the dermis and {{scars}}.
cards.explain: Depth determines scarring.

cards.prompt: What is the high-yield first split for a red, scaly rash, and the clue for each side?
Match each epidermal cell to its job:
cards.explain: Four epidermal cell types and their functions.

cards.prompt: Which are recognized psoriasis triggers? (select all)
cards.explain: Strep (guttate), drugs (beta-blockers, lithium, NSAIDs, steroid withdrawal), and Koebner trauma trigger psoriasis; sunlight usually helps.

cards.prompt: Why examine the nails and joints in a psoriasis patient?
cards.answer: Nail disease (pitting, onycholysis) is common and flags risk of psoriatic arthritis, which should be screened and referred early to prevent joint damage.

cards.prompt: Distinguish impetiginized eczema from eczema herpeticum.
cards.answer: Impetiginized eczema: weeping with honey crust (S. aureus) → antibiotics + eczema control. Eczema herpeticum: painful monomorphic vesicles/punched-out erosions + fever (HSV) → urgent systemic antivirals.

cards.text: Atopic dermatitis is driven by skin {{barrier}} dysfunction and immune dysregulation. Backbone of care is daily {{emollients}} plus topical steroids matched to site; use {{calcineurin}} inhibitors for sensitive areas.
cards.explain: Barrier repair + anti-inflammatory therapy; don't undertreat from steroid fear.

cards.prompt: Allergic contact dermatitis is which type of hypersensitivity?
cards.explain: Delayed type IV; confirmed by patch testing; common allergens include nickel, poison ivy, fragrances, neomycin/bacitracin.

cards.prompt: What drives stasis dermatitis and what is the key treatment?
cards.answer: Venous hypertension/insufficiency (edema). Key treatment is compression + leg elevation to address the cause, plus topical steroid for inflammation and wound care.

cards.prompt: Match the papulosquamous disease to its hallmark:
cards.explain: Pattern hallmarks for the well-demarcated red-and-scaly diseases.

cards.prompt: What finding signals necrotizing fasciitis, and what is the management?
cards.answer: Pain out of proportion to exam, rapid progression, systemic toxicity (± bullae/crepitus). It is a surgical emergency: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics + immediate debridement; don't delay surgery for imaging.

cards.text: {{Honey}}-coloured crusts are classic for impetigo (usually {{S. aureus}}); limited disease is treated with topical {{mupirocin}} (or fusidic acid).
cards.explain: Honey crust → impetigo → topical antibiotic for limited disease.

cards.prompt: Tinea capitis should be treated with:
cards.explain: Hair-follicle involvement means topical therapy is inadequate — oral antifungal is required. Same for nails and extensive disease.

cards.prompt: How do pityriasis versicolor and vitiligo differ?
cards.answer: Versicolor (Malassezia) gives hypo- or hyperpigmented finely scaly patches, KOH positive, treated with antifungals. Vitiligo is fully depigmented (not hypopigmented), non-scaly, and autoimmune.

cards.prompt: Match each viral lesion to its clue:
cards.explain: Morphology distinguishes the cutaneous viral infections.

cards.text: Scabies causes intense {{nocturnal}} itch with {{burrows}} in the finger webs and wrists; treat the patient and {{contacts}} with permethrin or ivermectin and decontaminate linens.
cards.explain: Itch + burrows + affected contacts; treat everyone and the environment.

cards.prompt: What is the classic morphology of tinea corporis, and what is 'tinea incognito'?
cards.answer: Annular scaly plaque with central clearing and an advancing scaly border. Tinea incognito is tinea altered/worsened by prior topical steroid use, losing its typical features.

cards.prompt: What single feature separates acne from rosacea?
cards.answer: Comedones — acne has them, rosacea does not (both can have papules/pustules).

cards.prompt: First-line for purely comedonal acne is:
cards.explain: Topical retinoids are comedolytic and target the microcomedone precursor — first-line for comedonal acne and maintenance.

cards.prompt: Which acne therapies are contraindicated/avoided in pregnancy? (select all)
cards.explain: Tetracyclines, retinoids/isotretinoin, and spironolactone are avoided in pregnancy; benzoyl peroxide is acceptable.

cards.prompt: Why should topical antibiotics not be used as monotherapy for acne?
cards.answer: Resistance develops quickly — combine with benzoyl peroxide (which lowers resistance) and/or a retinoid.

cards.text: For topical steroids, percent concentration {{does not}} equal potency. Clobetasol 0.05% is class {{1}} (super-potent); hydrocortisone 1% is class {{7}} (least potent). Use low potency on face, folds, genitals, and children.
cards.explain: Potency is by class and vehicle, not the percentage.

cards.prompt: Match the vehicle to its best use:
cards.explain: Vehicle choice depends on site and lesion.

cards.prompt: Which acne drug is teratogenic and requires pregnancy prevention + monitoring?
cards.answer: Isotretinoin — needs pregnancy prevention and β-hCG, plus lipid/LFT monitoring; for severe cystic/scarring or refractory acne.

cards.prompt: Match the skin cancer to its hallmark:
cards.explain: Recognition + behaviour for the key neoplasms.

cards.text: Suspected melanoma needs an {{excisional}} biopsy to measure {{Breslow}} depth; depth and {{ulceration}} are the dominant prognostic factors.
cards.explain: Don't shave/cryo a suspected melanoma — excise to get depth.

cards.prompt: What does ABCDE stand for?
cards.answer: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution.

cards.prompt: What is an actinic keratosis and why does it matter?
cards.answer: A rough, gritty premalignant keratinocyte lesion on sun-damaged skin; it can progress to squamous cell carcinoma. Treat with cryotherapy/field therapy and sun protection.

cards.prompt: What should you do if pathology does not fit the clinical impression?
cards.answer: Re-biopsy or pursue clinicopathologic correlation/discussion — pathology must correlate with the clinical picture, and margins matter in malignancy.

cards.prompt: Order these drug reactions from least to most severe:
cards.explain: Simple exanthem (no systemic) < DRESS (organ involvement) < SJS/TEN (mucosa, desquamation, mortality).

cards.text: DRESS begins about {{2-8 weeks}} after a drug with fever, facial edema, {{eosinophilia}}, and organ involvement. SJS/TEN shows tender skin, {{mucosal}} involvement, and a positive Nikolsky sign.
cards.explain: Timing and systemic features separate the serious drug reactions.

cards.prompt: Which drugs are classic culprits for SJS/TEN and DRESS? (select all)
cards.explain: Sulfonamides, anticonvulsants, allopurinol, and NSAIDs are common triggers.

cards.prompt: Match the feature to the bullous disease:
cards.explain: BP = subepidermal/tense; pemphigus = intraepidermal/flaccid.

cards.prompt: What is the HSP (IgA vasculitis) tetrad, and what must always be monitored?
cards.answer: Palpable purpura, arthralgia/arthritis, abdominal pain, and renal disease. Always monitor renal involvement with urinalysis and blood pressure.

cards.text: Painful skin + {{mucosal}} involvement + a new drug = {{SJS/TEN}} until proven otherwise; pain out of proportion = {{necrotizing fasciitis}}.
cards.explain: The two dermatologic emergencies to flag instantly.

cards.prompt: What is the most common trigger of acute urticaria in children?
cards.answer: Upper respiratory infection (URI). Treat with second-generation antihistamines; assess for angioedema/anaphylaxis.

cards.text: Infantile hemangiomas appear in the first weeks, proliferate for about {{6 months}}, then {{involute}} over years. Oral {{propranolol}} is used for large/high-risk lesions, especially near the eye.
cards.explain: Proliferate then involute; propranolol for high-risk lesions.

cards.prompt: How do scarring and non-scarring alopecia differ on exam and prognosis?
cards.answer: Non-scarring preserves follicular openings and can regrow; scarring loses follicular openings (stem cells destroyed) and cannot regrow in scarred areas — so treat early.

cards.text: Telogen effluvium classically sheds hair about {{3 months}} after an illness or stressor; screen for triggers such as low {{iron}} and {{thyroid}} disease.
cards.explain: Diffuse, reversible shed with a lag after the trigger.

cards.prompt: Match the alopecia to a feature/treatment:
cards.explain: Non-scarring types can regrow; scarring cannot.

cards.prompt: In pediatric atopic dermatitis, is broad food elimination recommended?
cards.answer: No — food elimination is usually unhelpful unless there is a true, confirmed allergy. Focus on emollients and appropriate-potency topical steroids (low potency on face/infants).

cards.prompt: What is botulinum toxin's mechanism?
cards.answer: It cleaves SNAP-25, preventing synaptic vesicle fusion and acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction.

cards.prompt: Which are benign mimickers to recognize (but biopsy if evolving/atypical)? (select all)
cards.explain: Seborrheic keratosis, dermatofibroma, and angiomas are benign; melanoma is malignant. CTCL can mimic chronic eczema/psoriasis.

cards.prompt: A limited plaque-psoriasis patient is best started on:
cards.explain: Limited disease: emollients + topical steroid and vitamin D analogue; escalate to phototherapy/systemics for extensive disease or psoriatic arthritis.

cards.prompt: Where does herpes simplex virus remain latent, and what does early antiviral therapy achieve?
cards.answer: In the dorsal root (sensory) ganglion. Early antivirals reduce the duration/severity of an outbreak but do not prevent all future recurrences.

guides.title: Week 10 · 1 Morphology & Approach
guides.html:
Language of Derm

Morphology & Approach

Week 10 · Dermatology
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The descriptive scaffold for every rash, the primary/secondary lesion vocabulary, and the first high-yield split — papulosquamous (well-demarcated) vs dermatitic (ill-defined).

Describing a rash

Order: primary morphology → size → colour → arrangement → distribution → secondary change → symptoms/time course. Accurate description makes most diagnoses fall out.

Skin functions: barrier (mechanical/chemical/UV/pathogen/dehydration), thermoregulation, vitamin D synthesis. Epidermis cells: keratinocytes (dominant), melanocytes (melanin), Langerhans (antigen presentation), Merkel (touch).

Primary lesions

TermRecognitionExample
Macule / patchFlat colour change; macule <1 cm, patch >1 cmFreckle, vitiligo
Papule / plaqueRaised; papule <1 cm, plaque >1 cm (scale raises it)Acne papule, psoriasis plaque
Nodule / tumourRaised with deeper component (“tumour” ≠ cancer)Nodular BCC, hemangioma
Vesicle / bullaFluid-filled; vesicle <1 cm, bulla >1 cmHSV, pemphigoid
Pustule / abscessPus-filled; abscess is deeperAcne pustule, furuncle

Secondary change

Scale = excess stratum corneum; crust = dried serum/pus/blood. Erosion (epidermal loss, heals without scar) vs ulcer (into dermis, scars). Lichenification = thickening from chronic rubbing; excoriation = scratch marks.

The first split: papulosquamous vs dermatitic

PatternCore clueKey disorders
PapulosquamousRed + scaly + well-demarcatedPsoriasis, lichen planus, pityriasis rosea, cutaneous lupus, tinea
DermatiticRed + scaly but ill-defined; itchy/oozy/crusted/lichenifiedAtopic, allergic contact, stasis dermatitis
Exam pearl
Well-demarcated + scale = papulosquamous until proven otherwise; ill-defined + itchy/oozy = dermatitic.

guides.title: Week 10 · 2 Papulosquamous & Dermatitic
guides.html:
Red & Scaly

Papulosquamous & Dermatitic

Week 10 · Dermatology
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Psoriasis and its triggers/comorbidities, atopic dermatitis with its two can't-miss complications, contact and stasis dermatitis, and the lichen planus / pityriasis rosea / lupus trio.

Psoriasis

Chronic, genetic, ~2% of population (peaks in 20s and 50s); relapses rather than cures. Well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale on scalp, elbows, knees, trunk; check nails (pitting → psoriatic arthritis risk).

Triggers
Streptococcal infection (guttate), stress, Koebner/trauma, cold, smoking/alcohol; drugs: beta-blockers, lithium, NSAIDs, steroid withdrawal.

Management: emollients; topical steroids + vitamin D analogues (limited); phototherapy (extensive); systemics/biologics (severe or psoriatic arthritis). Comorbidities: psoriatic arthritis, metabolic syndrome, CVD — screen joints.

Atopic dermatitis

Ill-defined, scaly, intensely pruritic; infants face/extensors, older flexures; more violaceous/hyperpigmented in darker skin. Driver: barrier dysfunction + immune dysregulation + atopy.

Management: daily emollients, gentle care, avoid irritants, topical steroids matched to site/severity, topical calcineurin inhibitors for sensitive areas/maintenance. Don't undertreat from steroid fear.

Two complications to recognize
Impetiginized eczema — weeping, honey crust, S. aureus → antibiotics + eczema control.
Eczema herpeticum — painful monomorphic vesicles / punched-out erosions, fever, rapid spread → urgent systemic antivirals + ophtho if eye.

Allergic contact dermatitis

Delayed type IV hypersensitivity. Itch, vesicles/bullae in a distribution matching exposure. Allergens: nickel, poison ivy, hair dye, fragrances, preservatives, neomycin/bacitracin. Dx: patch testing. Rx: avoidance + topical steroids (systemic if widespread).

Stasis dermatitis

Sign of venous hypertension: lower legs, edema, oozing, hemosiderin (brown) pigment, varicosities, ulcer risk. Treat the cause — compression + elevation; topical steroid for inflammation; wound care.

Lichen planus · pityriasis rosea · lupus

DiseaseRecognitionCourse / Rx
Lichen planusThe 6 Ps: pruritic, purple, polygonal, peripheral, papules, penile; Wickham striae; oral/nailTopical steroids; check mucosa/nails
Pityriasis roseaHerald patch → salmon plaques in Christmas-tree pattern, collarette scale, spares face; HHV-6/7Self-limited 6–8 wk; reassurance
Cutaneous lupusAcute malar rash sparing nasolabial folds, photosensitive; discoid scarsSun protection, topical steroids, antimalarials

guides.title: Week 10 · 3 Infections & Infestations
guides.html:
Bugs

Infections & Infestations

Week 10 · Dermatology
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Bacterial (impetigo → necrotizing fasciitis), viral (HSV/VZV/molluscum/warts), the tinea family with the capitis = oral-antifungal rule, pityriasis versicolor vs vitiligo, and scabies.

Bacterial

ConditionRecognitionManagement
ImpetigoHoney-coloured crust, face, S. aureus; contagiousRemove crusts; topical mupirocin/fusidic acid; oral cloxacillin/cephalosporin if extensive
Cellulitis / erysipelasWarm tender expanding plaque; portal of entry (tinea, abrasion)Antibiotics vs strep/S. aureus; elevate; treat portal
Necrotizing fasciitisPain out of proportion, rapid spread, systemic toxicity, late bullae/crepitusSurgical emergency — broad IV antibiotics + immediate debridement

Viral

  • HSV-1/2: grouped vesicles on erythematous base; latency in dorsal root ganglion; aciclovir/valaciclovir.
  • Varicella zoster: dermatomal grouped vesicles + pain; antivirals esp. early, facial/eye, immunocompromised.
  • Molluscum contagiosum: umbilicated dome papules; poxvirus; self-limited over 1–2 y; treat surrounding eczema.
  • Warts (HPV): rough papules with black dots, loss of dermatoglyphics; salicylic acid/cryo; HPV vaccine prevents high-risk types.

Fungal / yeast

Tinea
Dermatophyte of keratin. Corporis: annular plaque, central clearing + advancing border. Capitis: patchy alopecia, scale, broken hairs, lymphadenopathy → needs ORAL antifungal (topical inadequate). KOH/culture before prolonged systemic therapy. Beware tinea incognito from prior steroids.

Pityriasis versicolor: Malassezia overgrowth → hypo/hyperpigmented oval scaly patches in seborrheic distribution. Topical antifungals; oral azoles if extensive. Contrast vitiligo = fully depigmented, not hypopigmented.

Scabies

Pattern
Intense nocturnal itch, burrows, finger webs/wrists/genitals (feet in babies), affected contacts/congregate living.

Rx: permethrin cream or oral ivermectin + treat close contacts + decontaminate clothing/linens.

guides.title: Week 10 · 4 Acne, Rosacea & Therapeutics
guides.html:
Pilosebaceous & Rx

Acne, Rosacea & Therapeutics

Week 10 · Dermatology
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Acne by severity (and the comedone discriminator vs rosacea), the drug-caution table (isotretinoin teratogenicity, tetracycline limits), and topical therapeutics — vehicle, potency, and procedures.

Acne

Disorder of the pilosebaceous unit; true acne includes comedones (no comedones → think mimicker/drug). Pathogenesis: follicular hyperkeratinization, androgen-driven sebum, C. acnes, inflammation.

Rosacea vs acne: both have papules/pustules, but acne has comedones and rosacea does not — high-yield discriminator.

Match severity to therapy

PictureApproach
ComedonalTopical retinoid (comedolytic, targets the precursor)
Mild papulopustularTopical combo (retinoid + benzoyl peroxide ± antibiotic)
Moderate / truncalAdd oral antibiotic (3–6 mo, with topicals)
Severe cystic / scarringEarly isotretinoin
Adult female / PCOSAntiandrogen (OCP, spironolactone)

Acne drug cautions

DrugKey adverse / caution
Benzoyl peroxideLow resistance; dryness, bleaches fabric
Topical antibioticNever monotherapy (resistance) — pair with BPO
Topical retinoidIrritation, photosensitivity; avoid in pregnancy (tazarotene contraindicated)
Oral tetracyclinesContraindicated in pregnancy & children <8; photosensitivity, pseudotumor cerebri
SpironolactoneHyperkalemia, spotting; avoid pregnancy
IsotretinoinTeratogen — pregnancy prevention + β-hCG; monitor lipids/LFTs; cheilitis, mood

Topical therapeutics

Vehicle & potency
Ointment = greasy, most potent, dry thick plaques. Cream = elegant, drying. Lotion/foam/solution = hairy areas. Gel = spreads, may sting. Percent concentration ≠ potency (clobetasol 0.05% is class 1 super-potent; hydrocortisone 1% is class 7).

Steroid adverse effects: atrophy, striae, telangiectasia, perioral dermatitis, tachyphylaxis; absorption with high potency/large area/occlusion/children. Low potency on face, folds, genitals, children.

Procedures: cryotherapy (liquid N₂ −196°C, freeze-thaw); suspected melanoma → excisional biopsy; botulinum toxin cleaves SNAP-25 (blocks ACh release).

guides.title: Week 10 · 5 Skin Cancer & Mimickers
guides.html:
Neoplasia

Skin Cancer & Mimickers

Week 10 · Dermatology
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Actinic keratosis, the BCC vs SCC contrast, melanoma (ABCDE, excisional biopsy for Breslow depth), biopsy/clinicopathologic principles, and the benign mimickers including CTCL.

Actinic keratosis

Rough, gritty erythematous papule/thin plaque on sun-exposed skin; premalignant. Cumulative UV, Fitzpatrick I/II, bald scalp, immunosuppression. Cryotherapy (isolated) / field therapy; sun protection.

BCC & SCC

BCCSCC
LookPearly papule, telangiectasia, rolled border, may ulcer/bleedTender, keratotic, crusted, non-healing nodule; may arise from AK/wound
BehaviourMost common cancer; locally destructive, metastasis rareMore metastatic potential than BCC
RxBiopsy; excision/Mohs (high-risk); curettage/topical for superficialExcision or Mohs for high-risk sites

Melanoma

ABCDE
Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution. Acral/subungual can mimic bruise.

Excisional biopsy to assess Breslow depth (do not shave/cryo a suspected melanoma). Breslow depth and ulceration are the dominant prognostic features; wide local excision by depth ± sentinel node/imaging by stage.

Biopsy principles

Choose technique by lesion/site/info needed/pigment/preference. Margins matter in malignancy; pathology must correlate — if it doesn't fit the clinical picture, re-biopsy or clinicopathologic discussion.

Benign mimickers to recognize (avoid unnecessary procedures, but biopsy if evolving/atypical): seborrheic keratosis, dysplastic nevus, dermatofibroma, angioma, pigmented BCC. Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma can mimic chronic eczema/psoriasis (patch → plaque/tumour → Sézary).

guides.title: Week 10 · 6 Dangerous Rashes & Emergencies
guides.html:
Can't-Miss

Dangerous Rashes & Emergencies

Week 10 · Dermatology
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The drug-rash hierarchy by timing (morbilliform → DRESS → SJS/TEN), the bullous pemphigoid vs pemphigus discriminator, and IgA vasculitis with its mandatory renal monitoring.

Drug-rash hierarchy

ReactionTiming & cluesAction
Morbilliform exanthemMeasles-like, ~7–10 d after drug; no systemic involvement (amoxicillin + EBV)Stop drug, supportive; screen for danger signs
DRESS / DHS2–8 weeks after drug; fever, facial edema, morbilliform rash, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia, hepatitis/organStop drug; CBC/diff, LFTs, Cr, UA; systemic steroids; long taper
SJS / TENPainful/dusky skin + mucosa, atypical targets, bullae, +Nikolsky, desquamation, fever→shock. SJS <10% BSA, TEN >30%Stop drug; ICU/burn care; derm + ophtho

Common culprits: TMP-SMX, anticonvulsants, allopurinol, NSAIDs.

Bullous disease discriminator

Bullous pemphigoidPemphigus vulgaris
WhoElderly, intensely pruriticMiddle-aged; rarer
BullaeTense on normal/urticarial skin; mucosa sparedFlaccid, erosions; starts in mouth
NikolskyNegativePositive
TargetBasement membrane → subepidermalDesmogleins → intraepidermal acantholysis

IgA vasculitis (HSP)

Tetrad — and the thing to monitor
Palpable purpura (legs/buttocks), arthralgia/arthritis, abdominal pain, renal disease. Most common childhood systemic vasculitis; often post-URI. Always monitor renal — urinalysis + blood pressure.
Painful skin + mucosa + new drug
= SJS/TEN until proven otherwise. Severe pain out of proportion = necrotizing fasciitis. These are the dermatologic emergencies.

guides.title: Week 10 · 7 Pediatric Derm & Alopecia
guides.html:
Kids & Hair

Pediatric Derm & Alopecia

Week 10 · Dermatology
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Pediatric pearls (URI-triggered urticaria, infantile hemangioma and propranolol, molluscum), and alopecia anchored on the scarring vs non-scarring split with telogen effluvium triggers.

Pediatric dermatology

  • Childhood acute urticaria: wheals, each lesion <24 h; most common trigger is URI. 2nd-gen antihistamines; assess for angioedema/anaphylaxis.
  • Infantile hemangioma: appears first weeks, proliferates ~6 months, then involutes over years. Most just observed; oral propranolol for large/high-risk (esp. near eye), ulceration, or functional threat.
  • Molluscum: umbilicated papules, common in atopic children; self-limited; treat surrounding eczema.
  • Atopic dermatitis (peds): emollients + appropriate-potency topical steroid (low potency face/infants); food elimination usually unhelpful unless true allergy.

Alopecia — the key split

Scarring vs non-scarring
Non-scarring preserves follicular openings → can regrow. Scarring loses follicular openings (stem cells destroyed) → no regrowth in scarred areas — treat early to preserve remaining follicles.
TypeCluesRx
AndrogeneticMiniaturized hairs; men temples/vertex, women diffuse with preserved hairlineMinoxidil; finasteride (men)
Alopecia areataAutoimmune circular patches; stem cells intactTopical/intralesional steroids; regrowth possible
Telogen effluviumDiffuse shed ~3 months after illness/stressor (~20% of hair)Treat trigger; screen iron/thyroid; regrows
Scarring (LPP, CCCA)Lost follicular openings, perifollicular erythema/scaleShut down inflammation early; transplant only when quiet
